Authority bias trigger.definition: invokes or impersonates an authority figure (e.g., a senior executive, official organization, or institutional source)

Scarcity bias trigger.definition: emphasizes limited availability, exclusivity, or restricted access
examples: selected as one of only 10 users

Urgency bias trigger.definition: conveys time pressure or imminent negative consequences

Anchoring effect trigger.definition: highlights a numerical value to increase perceived legitimacy and prompt compliance.

Conformity bias trigger.definition: suggests others have already taken an action or that a behavior is the norm

Overconfidence trigger.definition: appeals to the recipient’s perceived expertise or competence
examples: Based on your advanced technical profile
your high compliance with company protocols

Familiarity bias trigger.definition: references known individuals, organizations, or contexts
